description: This figure presents finite element simulation results for a low-density steel (Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B, labeled "Our LDS") containing δ-ferrite, austenite, and TiB₂ phases, compared with conventional Fe-TiB₂ steel and interstitial-free (IF) steel. Panel (a) shows true stress versus true strain curves under damaged (hollow circles) and undamaged (solid lines) conditions, with experimental data (solid colored markers) closely matching simulations; "Our LDS" achieves the highest strength (~750 MPa), followed by Fe-TiB₂ steel (~610 MPa) and IF steel (~460 MPa). Panel (b) plots dislocation density evolution in the two matrix phases: δ-ferrite (red) starts higher (~11×10¹⁴ m⁻²) and saturates, while austenite (blue) starts lower (~3×10¹⁴ m⁻²) but rises rapidly, eventually exceeding δ-ferrite, consistent with experimental measurements (dots). Panel (c) illustrates stress partitioning among phases as functions of global true stress: TiB₂ (yellow) bears the highest stress (>1200 MPa), followed by δ-ferrite (red) and austenite (blue). At a global stress of ~490 MPa, the rate change indicates austenite yields first, while δ-ferrite remains elastic and carries more load. These results validate the constitutive model and demonstrate that the heterogeneous triplex microstructure enables superior strength through uneven stress distribution and sequential yielding.
